data.title: Phillips Edison Earnings Call Highlights Steady Growth
data.description:
Phillips Edison & Company, Inc. ((PECO)) has held its Q1 earnings call. Read on for the main highlights of the call.

Claim 30% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Discover top-performing stock ideas and upgrade to a portfolio of market leaders with Smart Investor Picks

Phillips Edison & Company, Inc. struck an upbeat tone on its latest earnings call, underscoring steady growth in funds from operations, strong leasing, and a fortified balance sheet. Management acknowledged macro and market risks, but emphasized raised FFO guidance, healthy liquidity, and resilient necessity-based tenant demand as reasons for confidence in the company’s outlook.

FFO Growth Signals Solid Earnings Momentum

NAREIT FFO per share increased 4.7% in the first quarter, while core FFO per share climbed 6.2% year over year, reflecting both internal growth and external investment. Updated 2026 guidance now implies NAREIT FFO per share growth of about 5.9% and core FFO growth of roughly 5.8% versus 2025 at the midpoint, in line with the REIT’s long-term targets.

Same-Center NOI and Leasing Execution Remain Robust

Same-center net operating income rose 3.5% in the quarter, supported by very high portfolio occupancy of 97.1%, with anchors at 98.4% and inline space at 95%. Leasing spreads showcased pricing power, with comparable renewals up 21.2%, new leases up 36.2%, and inline deals embedding average annual rent bumps of 2.7%.

Balance Sheet Strengthens with Ample Liquidity

The company ended the quarter with $810 million of liquidity and completed a $350 million public bond offering at 4.75% due 2033, extending duration at attractive terms. Debt is largely insulated from rate swings, with a 4.4% weighted average interest rate, 5.8-year average maturity including extensions, and 94% of total debt fixed-rate.

Acquisitions and Development Drive External Growth

Year to date, Phillips Edison has deployed $185 million across five grocery-anchored centers, three everyday retail centers, and land, with about $150 million more under contract or awarded and expected to close by the end of the second quarter. On the development side, 19 projects under construction represent roughly $74 million of investment with estimated 9%–12% yields, and six projects stabilized in the quarter, adding about 87,000 square feet and $1.7 million of annual NOI.

Bad Debt Comes in Below Expectations

Credit performance was a tailwind, as bad debt came in at roughly 60 basis points of revenue in the first quarter, below the 2025 full-year expectation of 78 basis points. Management still expects 2026 bad debt levels to be broadly in line with 2025, suggesting a cautious but stable view on tenant credit quality.

Pricing Power Underpinned by Necessity Retail

Management highlighted strong retailer demand across necessity-based categories, with about 74% of rents tied to goods and services that consumers need regardless of the cycle. That mix underpins what the company characterizes as market-leading pricing power, supported by a robust renewal pipeline with 125 deals already out for signature.

Guidance Raised and Growth Targets Reaffirmed

Phillips Edison raised its 2026 FFO guidance ranges, now pointing to mid-to-high single-digit NAREIT and core FFO per-share growth, backed by first-quarter outperformance and a solid capital position. At the same time, it reaffirmed expectations for 3%–4% same-center NOI growth and $400 million–$500 million of gross acquisitions on a company-share basis, reinforcing confidence in both operations and deal flow.

Asset Recycling Supports Capital Flexibility

The REIT has completed $29 million of dispositions year to date on a company-share basis and plans to continue selling select assets to fund higher-return opportunities. Management emphasized a diversified capital toolkit that includes debt issuance, joint ventures, and potential equity issuance when market conditions are favorable, aiming to balance growth with leverage discipline.

Macro and Rate Volatility Remain Key Watchpoints

Executives acknowledged a backdrop of interest rate volatility, shifting global trade patterns, geopolitical conflict, election-related uncertainty, and elevated energy costs as factors that could influence markets and capital allocation. These risks are being weighed in decisions on timing of debt issuance, acquisition pacing, and overall balance-sheet positioning.

Public vs. Private Valuation Gap Adds Pressure

Management noted a 50–75 basis point spread between private and public market pricing, which currently favors private buyers and complicates capital sourcing for listed REITs. That gap can contribute to public market discounts versus intrinsic value and heightens the importance of disciplined capital allocation and accretive external growth.

Leverage Slightly Elevated but Monitored

Net debt to trailing twelve-month annualized adjusted EBITDAR stood at 5.3 times at quarter end, or 5.1 times on a last-quarter annualized basis, slightly above some peers and the company’s historical range. Management indicated that leverage is being closely monitored as it continues to pursue acquisitions and development, suggesting a focus on maintaining balance-sheet flexibility.

Retention Headline Masked by One Large Vacancy

The reported quarterly retention rate of 88% was dragged down by a single planned 64,000 square foot box vacancy, which had been anticipated. Excluding that known move-out, the retention rate would have been roughly 92.4%, highlighting both the resilience of the broader tenant base and the ongoing exposure to occasional large-box churn.

Credit Collectibility Guidance Stays Cautious

Despite better-than-expected bad debt in the quarter, the company maintained its collectibility adjustment guidance at $5 million to $8 million and reiterated that 2026 bad debt should track roughly in line with 2025. This stance reflects management’s conservative approach to credit risk, even as underlying collections trends remain solid.

Execution Risk in Non-Core Everyday Retail Push

Phillips Edison is leaning further into unanchored everyday retail and land development, targeting higher unlevered returns of roughly 9%–11% with acquisition cap rates around 6.5%–6.9%. While these investments could enhance growth and returns, they introduce added execution and market risk compared with the firm’s traditional grocery-anchored focus.

Guidance and Outlook Underscore Confident Growth Path

Looking ahead, the company’s updated 2026 outlook calls for NAREIT FFO per share to grow about 5.9% and core FFO roughly 5.8% versus 2025 at the midpoint, consistent with its mid-to-high single-digit long-term ambitions. With 3%–4% same-center NOI growth, $400 million–$500 million in planned acquisitions, strong liquidity, mostly fixed-rate debt, and steady bad debt assumptions, management signaled confidence in sustaining growth despite macro headwinds.

Phillips Edison’s latest earnings call painted a picture of a necessity-focused retail REIT executing well on both operations and capital deployment, with raised FFO guidance reinforcing that story. While investors must weigh macro uncertainty, valuation gaps, and execution risk in new strategies, the company’s high occupancy, strong leasing spreads, and ample liquidity position it as a steady compounder in a volatile market.

Value-Added Services Momentum

Value‑added services revenue increased 22% year over year to $201 million, outpacing total company growth and underscoring strong demand for ancillary products. Core subscription revenue also performed well, rising 18% to $58 million on the back of new wins, unit growth, and greater adoption of higher‑priced Plus and Max tiers.

High AI Adoption and Product Traction

Management emphasized that more than 99% of roughly 23,000 customers now use some part of the Realm AI suite, with AI actions up sevenfold year over year. Performer adoption surged nearly 500% quarter over quarter, while over 5 million units now connect via AppFolio Stack and early tools like Maintenance Performer and Resident Onboarding Lift show strong engagement and efficiency gains.

Operational Efficiency Gains

Operating discipline remained a key theme, with R&D spending falling to 16% of revenue from 17% and G&A dropping to 7% from 8%, while sales and marketing held steady at 13%. The company credited AI‑native engineering practices and higher team productivity for enabling faster product delivery without sacrificing cost efficiency.

Disciplined Capital Allocation

AppFolio continued to return capital to shareholders, deploying $125 million in Q1 to repurchase 702,500 shares and bringing total buybacks in 2025–2026 to nearly 1.4 million shares. Management framed the repurchases as part of a balanced capital allocation strategy aimed at managing dilution while still investing in growth initiatives.

Cost Pressure from Payments and AI Infrastructure

Cost of revenue excluding depreciation and amortization held flat at 36% of revenue, as favorable trends were offset by payments mix and higher data center investment to support AI usage. Executives cautioned that if payments behavior or AI consumption shifts, these factors could pressure gross margins even as they underpin long‑term product differentiation.

Dependence on Value-Added and Premium Tiers

A growing portion of AppFolio’s expansion is tied to value‑added services and premium Plus and Max tiers, with value‑added revenue alone hitting $201 million, up 22% year over year. Management acknowledged that a slowdown in these higher‑margin offerings could weigh on both future revenue growth and margin performance, making continued upsell momentum critical.

Revenue Growth Outpacing Unit Expansion

With units up 8% but revenue rising 20%, the company is increasingly driven by average revenue per unit rather than pure volume growth. While this ARPU‑led model boosts profitability, it also heightens exposure to any softening in demand for upgrades and add‑ons if customers become more cost conscious.

Share Repurchases and Investment Balance

The $125 million Q1 repurchase represents a meaningful use of cash, directly supporting earnings per share by reducing the share count. At the same time, such deployment limits near‑term dry powder for larger strategic deals, underscoring management’s conviction that reinvesting in the business and buying back stock currently offer the best risk‑reward.

Guidance and Outlook

Looking ahead to 2026, AppFolio now forecasts revenue between $1.11 billion and $1.125 billion, implying about 17.5% growth at the midpoint, and expects non‑GAAP operating margins of 26%–28%. The company sees cost of revenue excluding depreciation staying roughly flat as a percentage of sales, modest operating‑expense leverage despite continued hiring, largely unchanged seasonality, and an average diluted share count of about 36 million.

AppFolio’s earnings call painted the picture of a software company leaning into AI, premium tiers, and value‑added services to drive faster growth and thicker margins. While payments mix, infrastructure costs, and reliance on upselling introduce some risk, the raised guidance and strong adoption metrics suggest management believes its growth playbook remains firmly intact.

Narrowing Losses and Emerging Operating Leverage

SES AI’s GAAP net loss narrowed to $12.1 million, or $0.04 per share, from $17.0 million, or $0.05 per share, while non‑GAAP net loss improved to $11.1 million, or $0.03 per share. Adjusted EBITDA loss improved to $12.8 million from $13.8 million, reflecting early signs of operating leverage as revenue scales faster than core costs.

Liquidity Strength and Cash Burn Dynamics

The company ended Q1 with roughly $178 million in liquidity while using about $20 million of cash in operations during the quarter, in line with its plan. Management emphasized its capital‑expenditure‑light model and believes current liquidity is sufficient to fund key growth initiatives through 2026, though sustained burn underscores the need to keep tightening expenses.

ATGE Deal Opens North American ESS Channel

In energy storage systems, SES AI signed a multiyear distribution agreement with ATGE Power to enter the North American market, valued at about $20 million over three years. The deal gives immediate access to established residential, commercial, and industrial channels, potentially accelerating adoption of the company’s UZ ESS products.

Drone Cell Manufacturing and Early Demand Pipeline

SES AI completed the conversion of its Jeongju, South Korea line to produce drone‑format power cells, planning to ramp capacity to more than one million units annually. It also began shipping NDA‑compliant cells from Chengdu for customer evaluation, with a pipeline of a few dozen prospects and indicative pricing of about $25 to $35 per cell, implying $25 million to $35 million potential revenue at full utilization.

Molecular Universe Platform Gains Commercial Traction

The company launched Molecular Universe v2.5, its fifth major upgrade, adding new AI‑driven workflows and on‑premise deployment options for customers. A major global battery manufacturer signed a multiyear subscription for the Search‑in‑a‑Box product, while around half a dozen customers moved into second‑phase materials testing and the Hyzon joint venture remained on track for large‑scale production.

Finance Leadership Transition for Next Growth Phase

SES AI announced a CFO transition, with Jing Nealis stepping down and Ray Liu, a CFA and CPA, taking over the role. Management presented the move as a way to support the next phase of scaling, aiming to preserve continuity in financial strategy while sharpening focus on profitability and capital allocation.

Profitability Still Distant Despite Progress

Despite the improvements, SES AI remains firmly in the red, with GAAP net loss at $12.1 million, non‑GAAP loss at $11.1 million, and adjusted EBITDA negative $12.8 million in Q1. Elevated GAAP operating expenses nearly triple revenue and the business remains dependent on scaling sales and executing cost cuts to approach breakeven.

Current NDA‑compliant capacity is largely in Korea and Chengdu, and management is exploring Southeast Asia options, meaning capacity limits could cap near‑term revenue if demand ramps quickly.
